coredata.dc:title: Dual emissive carbon dots with one-pot synthesized and their tunable luminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yaoyao
Min, Xiao
Hu, Mengzhu
Liu, Bingfa
Wu, Yuheng
Wu, Yuansheng
Yu, Lixin
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a dual photoluminescence (PL) carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are obtained using two common raw materials, phenylenediamines and phosphorus acid, with a one-step hydrothermal method. The three typical CQDs are obtained by using three phenylenediamine isomers as carbon source, which can emit a bright and stable turquoise, green and yellow light under a single ultraviolet-light excitation, respectively. In addition, the effect of reaction temperature and ambient pH on the performance of CQDs is systematically researched in the hydrothermal process. The results show that tunable optical properties of CQDs can be achieved by changing the reaction temperature, carbon sources and the surrounding pH value.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Hydrothermal method
Tunable emission

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of oCDs, mCDs, pCDs.
a–c) TEM images (top) of oCDs, mCDs, pCDs obtained at 180℃ and their corresponding size distribution histograms (bottom).

(A–C) PL emission spectra under diﬀ ;erent excitation wavelengths, (D) digital photographs under 365 nm UV lamp, (E) corresponding normalized PL emission spectra at 380 nm excitation, (F) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of oCDs, mCDs and pCDs.
The PL emission spectra under 380 nm excitation of A) oCDs, B) mCDs and C) pCDs, D) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of pCDs, obtained under 140, 160, 180, 200℃.
The PL emission spectra under 380 nm excitation for A) oCDs, B) mCDs and C) pCDs, in different pH condition obtained at 180℃.

head.ce:title: Dual emissive carbon dots with one-pot synthesized and their tunable luminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Road, Nanchang 330031, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a dual photoluminescence (PL) carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are obtained using two common raw materials, phenylenediamines and phosphorus acid, with a one-step hydrothermal method. The three typical CQDs are obtained by using three phenylenediamine isomers as carbon source, which can emit a bright and stable turquoise, green and yellow light under a single ultraviolet-light excitation, respectively. In addition, the effect of reaction temperature and ambient pH on the performance of CQDs is systematically researched in the hydrothermal process. The results show that tunable optical properties of CQDs can be achieved by changing the reaction temperature, carbon sources and the surrounding pH value.

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs are a novel quasi spherical carbon nanoparticle composed of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen and other elements, which are made up of carbon cores with a large number of oxygen-containing groups on the surface, and the particle size is typically less than 10 nm [1]. The covalent carbon core provides a stable structure for CQDs, while the surrounding abundant functional groups play an important role in their excellent water-solubility, PL performance and quantum yield (QY) properties [2,3]. Due to its unique structure, CQDs possess many excellent characteristics of adjustable excitation and emission wavelength range [4], good optical stability and high fluorescence intensity, which makes CQDs have potential application value in biochemical sensing [5], drug delivery [6], biomedical [7,8], hotoelectric devices [9,10] and ion detection [11], etc.

Since the accidental discovery of CQDs in 2004 [12], a variety of studies reported the luminescence of CQDs. Light in yellow region is getting more and more attention due to the maximum luminous sensation of the human visual. Most of reports include the phosphoric acid treatment and the use of phenylenediamine as the precursor [13,14]. Meanwhile, many synthetic methods have also been proposed including arc discharge [15], laser denudation [16], electrochemical oxidation [17], chemical oxidation [18], pyrolysis [19], Microwave synthesis [20] and so on. However, the preparation process of these method require many complex instruments and extreme operating conditions, which may cause safety problems and waste of energy. Compared with these preparation methods, solvothermal synthesis has become a commonly used synthesis technique at present due to its simple, economical and green synthesis process and the higher QY. Furthermore, it’s luminescence mechanism has not been fully explained and still under intensive investigation. Therefore, it’s necessary to consider a tunable emission wavelength CQDs with comparable experimental parameters for further exploration.

Herein, a dual PL CQDs are obtained with a one-step hydrothermal method. The ﬂuorescence behavior of CQDs is found to be related to the different reaction temperatures, carbon sources and as well as the surrounding pH values. The emission of blue light is inhibited and the emission of yellow light is promoted with the increase of reaction temperature, while, the emission of yellow light is inhibited and the emission of blue light is promoted with increasing the pH value of precursor solution.

o-phenylenediamine (oPD, 98 %), m-phenylenediamine (mPD, 99 %) and p-phenylenediamine (pPD, 97 %) were purchased from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Phosphorus acid (85 wt. %) was obtained from XiLong Scientific Co., Ltd., Guangdong, China. Ethanol (AR) was purchased from DaMao Chemical Reagent Factory, Tianjin, China.

The CQDs are prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, phenylenediamine (0.1 g) and phosphorus acid (1 mL) are dissolved in 40 mL of ethanol. The above mixed solution is sealed in a Teﬂon-lined autoclave and maintaining at 180℃ for 24 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the autoclave is cooled down to room temperature naturally. The dark red solution is centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove impurities (This wash operation is repeated three times). Afterwards, the collected supernatant is filtered through a 0.22 μm organic microporous membrane. At last, the CQDs are obtained by dialysis, freeze drying and dispersed in water for further characterization.

The morphology and size of the as-synthesized CQDs are characterized by Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010, JEOL). Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer is selected to measure the PL excitation and emission spectra. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded by using HITACHI UH4150 spectrophotometer.

The optical properties of the three CQDs are analyzed by the UV–vis absorption and the fluorescent spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 , the CQDs obtained with oPD, mPD, pPD as carbon sources achieve the strong and stable turquoise, green and yellow luminescence with naked eyes under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3D), respectively. According to Fig. 3A, under different excitation wavelengths for oCDs, an emission band centered at 530 nm is observed and its position hardly changes, indicating that the size distribution is relative homogeneous. In addition, at 340 nm excitation, except for the emission at 530 nm, an addition emission at 415 nm is also observed. For mCDs, the rule of the dependence of excitation wavelength is same with the oCDs. For pCDs, under different excitation wavelengths, a green emission at 525 nm (340 nm), 537 nm (360 nm), 540 nm (380 nm), 544 nm (400 nm), 547 nm (420 nm) is observed, respectively. Moreover, at short wavelength (340, 360, 380 nm) excitation, a blue emission at 450 nm is also observe and their relative intensity (green to blue) dramatically varies. The relative intensity of blue emission becomes higher as the excitation wavelength is longer as the order of 340, 360, 380 nm. This result may be assigned to the size effect and surface state.

For the convenience of practical analysis, the emission behavior of oCDs, mCDs and pCDs under excitation of 380 nm is shown in Fig. 3E. Their strongest emission peak locates at 530 nm (oCDs), 512 nm (mCDs), 540 nm (pCDs), respectively. For oCDs and pCDs, except for green emission, blue emission also occurs and its intensity of pCDs is much stronger than that of oCDs. Furthermore, the UV–Vis spectra is employed to investigate the energy absorption of CQDs as shown in Fig. 3F. In the low energy region, the spectra display the distinct absorption bands at 380, 442 and 346 nm, which can be ascribed to changes of the functional groups on the CQDs surface, such as C = O, CN could be providing different surface states

To further explore the tunable emission of CQDs. The photoluminescence properties of oCDs, mCDs, pCDs are investigated at different reaction temperatures (140−200℃) and shown in Fig. 4 . As reaction temperature increased, the green emission from the oCDs (Fig. 4A) excited by 380 nm shows a slight blue shift. It should be noted that an addition emission peak at 425 nm occurs in oCDs at 200 ℃. For mCDs (Fig. 4B), only one green emission is observed and red shift as the hydrothermal temperature is increased. It is a significant result for elucidating the tunable emission of CQDs. For the convenience of practical analysis, the pCDs is selected to explore carefully. As shown in Fig. 4C, notably, the all as-prepared pCDs exhibit two distinctive emission peaks at around 450 and 540 nm, showing a dual emission characteristic, which is different from the commonly reported CQDs [21]. Moreover, the reaction temperature hardly affect the emission location. But the relative intensity obviously changes. As seen, the reaction is lower, the emission at 450 nm is stronger, implying that it can be obviously observed that the emission of blue light is decreased with the increase of reaction temperature. As known, when the reaction is increased, the crystallization of nanocrystal becomes better, indicating that the defect (including body and surface defects) is less. Based on the present results and analysis, the emission at 450 nm can be attributed to the surface state (including surface defects and absorbed functional groups). The temperature is increased, the surface defects become less. Furthermore, the pCDs energy absorption characteristic is investigated (as shown in Fig. 4D). Distinct from pCDs obtained at different reaction temperatures exhibit absorption peaks, which can be related to the changes of band gap due to the variety of oxygen-containing functional groups in the surface structure [21]. These results consistent with the above and demonstrated that the he tunable emission of CQDs is effect from reaction temperature.

As the reported in the previous section, the fluorescence behavior is influenced by the surface state, particle size and carbon nuclear structure. The degree of oxidation or surface state of CQDs can be controlled by tuning pH value of surrounding environment to obtain the more information. Therefore, the pH environment effect on CQDs is investigated by adding NaOH solution to adjust the the pH value of CQDs mixture to 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 and 13. As shown in Fig. 5 , the emission spectra of oCDs, mCDs, pCDs obtained at 180℃ under different pH environment at 380 nm excitation hardly change. It can be obviously observed that the emission wavelength of the three CQDs shows nearly no movement when the pH of solution surrounding environment is increased, which is different from the previous reported. Interestingly, a dual emission is observed at the emission spectra of mCDs. Compared with the obtained CQDs at different temperatures, pH promoted the emission of short wavelengths on the contrary. This result implies that the presence of H+ does not affect the surface effect and the size effect (no emission shift).

In summary, the CQDs were successfully prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method exploited phosphoric acid and phenylenediamine as the raw material. The prepared CQDs possessed dual PL emissive spectra and excellent luminescence properties. Furthermore, the emission of prepared CQDs influenced by the different reaction temperature, carbon sources and the surrounding pH value. It can be included that the higher temperature and the smaller pH will promote the long wavelength emission. We speculate that this phenomenon is the result of the combined eﬀ ;ect from phosphoric acid and phenylenediamines.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully thank the financial supports of Nation Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51762033), Graduate Student Innovation Special Funds of Jiangxi province (NO. YC2020-S067).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Long-wavelength, multicolor, and white-light emitting carbon-based dots: achievements made, challenges remaining, and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mojtaba; Shamsipur; Ali; Barati; Sara; Karami, Long-wavelength, multicolor, and white-light emitting carbon-based dots: Achievements made, challenges remaining, and applications. Carbon 2017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescence: synthesis of carbon dots with multiple color emission by controlled graphitization and surface functionalization (Adv. Mater. 1/2018)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Miao, X.; Qu, D.; Yang, D.; Nie, B.; Zhao, Y.; Fan, H.; Sun, Z., Photoluminescence: Synthesis of Carbon Dots with Multiple Color Emission by Controlled Graphitization and Surface Functionalization (Adv. Mater. 1/2018). Advanced Materials 2018, 30 (1), 1870002.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Risk factors for the long-term efficacy, recurrence, and metastasis in small hepatocellular carcinomas

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, Y.; Gao, S. G.; Chen, J. M.; Wang, G. P.; Wang, Z. F.; Zhou, B.; Jin, C. H.; Yang, Y. T.; Feng, X. S., Risk factors for the Long-Term Efficacy, Recurrence, and Metastasis in Small Hepatocellular Carcinomas. Cell Biochemistry&Biophysics 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jiang, K.; Sun, S.; Zhang, L.; Lu, Y.; Wu, A.; Cai, C.; Lin, H., Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging. Angew Chem Int Ed Engl 2015, 54 (18), 5360-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from one-step pyrolysis of frontal-polymerized poly(acrylamide-co-4-vinylpyridine)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, Q.-L.; Tang, W.-Q.; Wang, C.-F.; Chen, S., Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from one-step pyrolysis of frontal-polymerized poly(acrylamide-co-4-vinylpyridine). Applied Physics A 2014, 117 (3), 1583-1588.

M.; Wen, H., The role of laparoscopy in the management of liver hydatid cyst: a single-center experience and world review of the literature. Advanced Materials 2018.
